# jett.game — LLM Context File

> This file follows the emerging llms.txt standard (https://llmstxt.org).
> It provides a structured, token-efficient summary for AI language models.

## Project

- **Name:** jett.game
- **Type:** Mobile-first web casino game platform
- **Live:** https://dist-omega-henna.vercel.app
- **Repo:** https://github.com/zero-934/jett-game (private)
- **Owner:** C Lee (GitHub: zero-934) — first-time developer
- **Stack:** Phaser 3 + TypeScript (strict) + Vite + Jest
- **Deploy:** Vercel, auto-deploy on push to `main`
- **Aesthetic:** Midnight Luxury — charcoal #0d0d0d, gold #c9a84c

## Architecture

Every game = exactly 3 files:
- `src/games/<Name>Logic.ts` — pure TypeScript, no Phaser, all game math/RNG/state
- `src/games/<Name>UI.ts` — Phaser rendering + input, calls Logic only
- `src/scenes/<Name>Scene.ts` — wires Logic+UI, registered in Phaser config

New scenes must be added to `src/main.ts` scene array AND `HomeScene.ts` cards array.

## Games

1. **Jett** (JettScene) — vertical scroller, dodge asteroids, multiplier rises with altitude, combustion mechanic, cash-out anytime
2. **Shatter Step** (ShatterStepScene) — 10-row ladder, 50/50 L/R each row, multiplier ×1.5 per row, cash-out after any safe row
3. **Flap Fortune** (FlapFortuneScene) — horizontal scroller, flap through pipe gaps, distance multiplier, cash-out anytime
4. **Dice** (DiceScene) — single roll, pick 2×/5×/10× odds, instant result
5. **Mines** (MinesScene) — 5×5 grid, 3/5/10 bombs, reveal safe tiles, multiplier grows, cash-out anytime
6. **Ball Drop** (BallDropScene) — peg-board drop, player nudges ball mid-fall, edge slots ×5 / centre ×0.3

## Standards

- TypeScript strict mode enforced
- Every public function: JSDoc with @param, @returns, @example
- File header: @file, @purpose, @author, @date, @license
- RTP target: 96% (4% house edge) on all games
- House edge in Logic.ts only, never in UI or Scene
- Tests: Jest + ts-jest, no Phaser mocking needed (Logic is pure TS)

## Key Rules for AI Agents

- NEVER import Phaser in a Logic.ts file
- NEVER put RNG or payout math in UI.ts or Scene.ts
- ALWAYS add a test file for every new Logic.ts
- ALWAYS add the new scene to main.ts AND HomeScene.ts cards[]
- NEVER push to main directly — branch + PR only
- Branch: feat/<game-name> | PR title: feat(<game-name>): initial prototype

## Licensing & Business Plan

### Stage 1 (Immediate – Anjouan License)
- No physical office required. Remote operation from anywhere.
- No game certification needed for launch. Self-certify RNG via documentation.
- Focus: launch fast, generate revenue, prove game mechanics.
- Target markets: all of Africa, Asia, Latin America (geo-block US, UK, EU, Australia).

### Stage 2 (Future – Curaçao, Angola, or other Tier-1 license)
- Will require formal third-party RNG certification (GLI, iTech Labs, BMM).
- Each game’s math model, RTP, and payout logic will need lab testing.
- Platform-level RNG certification can cover all games (cheaper than per-game).
- Expect local presence requirements (office, staff) for some jurisdictions (e.g., Angola).

### Development rules to follow now (for easy future certification):
1. Separate game logic (RNG, math, payouts) from UI.
2. Use a central, well-documented RNG engine that can be certified once.
3. Document RTP and house edge for every game.
4. Follow GLI-11 standards where possible.
5. No hardcoded secrets; use environment variables.

Keep building. We’ll handle licensing details as we scale.

## File Locations

- AI agent rules: AGENTS.md (primary), AGENT_RULES.md (legacy)
- Entry point: src/main.ts
- Home screen: src/scenes/HomeScene.ts
- Game logic: src/games/*Logic.ts
- Game rendering: src/games/*UI.ts
- Scenes: src/scenes/*Scene.ts
- Tests: src/tests/*Logic.test.ts

## Commands

```bash
npm install       # install deps
npm run dev       # dev server (localhost:5173)
npm run build     # production build
npm test          # run all Jest tests
```

## Context Recovery (for AI agents)

Lost context? Do this:
1. Read AGENTS.md
2. Run: npm test
3. Read src/main.ts (scene list)
4. Read src/scenes/HomeScene.ts (game cards)
5. Read one Logic.ts to understand code style
6. Ask the user what to work on next
